[[File:Nagayu Onsen Ganiyu 1950s.jpg|thumb|right|Ganiyu (circa 1950)]]
'''Nagayu Onsen''' (長湯温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Taketa|ja|竹田市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft}}, in the former province of {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国|qq=draft}}. Due to its high concentration of {{ill|carbon dioxide|ja|炭酸|qq=draft}}, its abundant water flow, and optimal temperature, it is regarded as "one of the world's foremost carbonated springs"<ref>[http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen.com] Naoiri Town Tourism Association</ref> and "one of Japan's premier carbonated springs."<ref name="plan_takeda">{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/plan_takeda.pdf Taketa Hot Springs Group (Nagayu Onsen, Kuju Highland Onsen, Taketa-Ogi Onsen) National Health Resort Plan]}} Ministry of the Environment, May 2015</ref> It is particularly known for its health benefits, including for drinking therapy.
== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft}}<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Number of sources: 36<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Temperature: 24.6°C–52°C<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Water discharge: 16.1–290 liters per minute<ref name="plan_takeda" />
{{ill|Bicarbonate springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft}} can range in color from transparent to yellowish-white, depending on the source, while {{ill|Carbon dioxide springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft}} are generally colorless. Most facilities use a {{ill|free-flowing hot spring|ja|掛け流し|1=Kakenagashi|2=ja|3=掛け流し|4=zh|5=掛け流し|lt=Kakenagashi (flowing baths)|6=de|7=Kakenagashi|8=fr|9=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|qq=draft}} system.<ref name="onsen">[http://www.gozenyu.com/onsen.html Hot Spring Information] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref>
A study conducted in 1985 by {{ill|Kao Corporation|ja|花王|qq=draft}} identified Nagayu Onsen as one of Japan's leading carbonated springs.<ref name="plan_takeda" /><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012301.htm |title=Nagayu Story <1> Kao's Carbonated Spring Evaluation...Revitalizing the Town and Increasing Tourism Elevenfold |date=20100820235256}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 3, 2008</ref>
In 2006, the claim of being "Japan’s No.1 carbonated spring" was challenged due to insufficient scientific evidence, leading to a temporary retraction. However, a later evaluation in 2007 determined that while its CO₂ concentration was moderate, it had the highest discharge rate and optimal bathing temperature, leading to a reassertion of its claim.<ref name="na_08012305">{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012305.htm |title=Nagayu Story <5> "Japan’s Best" Reaffirmed...A Chance to Reevaluate Ourselves |date=20100826025904}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 8, 2008</ref>
== Medical Benefits ==
Although individual results may vary, the following benefits are attributed to Nagayu Onsen:
* Bathing: Beneficial for {{ill|diabetes|ja|糖尿病|qq=draft}}, {{ill|gastrointestinal diseases|ja|胃腸病|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|cardiovascular diseases|ja|心臓病|qq=draft}} * Drinking: Supports internal organ function<ref name="plan_takeda" />
== Onsen Town ==
[[File:Gozenyu.jpg|right|upright|thumb|Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"]] [[File:RamuneOnsenExterior.JPG|right|thumb|Ramune Onsen]]
The hot spring town is situated along the Serikawa River, surrounded by a rural landscape. Traditional inns and public bathhouses are scattered throughout the area.
=== Major Bathhouses ===
* '''Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"''' – Modern bathhouse built to resemble a European-style facility. Inspired by Nagayu’s sister-city partnership with Bad Krozingen, Germany. * '''Ramune Onsen''' – Known for its transparent, highly carbonated water. Designed by architect {{ill|Terunobu Fujimori|ja|藤森照信|qq=draft}}. * '''Ganiyu''' – Free outdoor bath visible from nearby roads and inns. * '''Choseiyu''' – Affordable public bath with an entrance fee of only 200 yen.
== History ==
* Nagayu Onsen is mentioned in the early 8th-century {{ill|Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|豊後国風土記|qq=draft}}.<ref name="plan_takeda" /> * 1706: A tea house for the {{ill|Oka Domain|ja|岡藩|qq=draft}} lord was constructed at the onsen.<ref name="nagayu_rekishi">[http://www.gozenyu.com/nagayu_rekishi.html History of Nagayu Onsen] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref> * 1978: Officially designated as a {{ill|National Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft}}. * 2015: Expanded designation as part of the {{ill|Taketa Hot Spring Group|ja|竹田温泉群|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/list.pdf National Health Resort List]}} Ministry of the Environment</ref>
== Access ==
* {{ill|Hōhi Main Line|ja|豊肥本線|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Bungotaketa Station|ja|豊後竹田駅|qq=draft}}, then {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft}} (40–50 minutes). * Limited bus services from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|阿蘇くまもと空港|qq=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of hot springs in Japan|ja|日本の温泉地一覧|1=List of hot springs in Japan|2=ja|3=日本の温泉地一覧|4=zh|5=日本溫泉列表|1=List of hot springs in Japan|2=ja|3=日本の温泉地一覧|4=zh|5=日本溫泉列表|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Carbon Dioxide Springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen Official Website] (Japanese)
{{translated page|ja|長湯温泉|version=104645983|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox hot spring | name = Nagayu Onsen | image = Ganiyu.JPG | caption = Ganiyu by the river {{Location map+|Japan Oita Prefecture|relief=1|width=300|caption=|float=center|places= {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 04| lat_sec = 0.4|lon_deg = 131| lon_min = 22| lon_sec = 25.4|position=right|label=Nagayu Onsen|label_size=100|caption=}} {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg=33|lat_min=05|lat_sec=09|lon_deg=131|lon_min=14|lon_sec=56|position=left|mark=3-fold rotation axis.svg|label={{ill|Mount Kujū|ja|九重山|qq=draft}}}} }} | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Taketa|ja|竹田市|qq=draft}}, Naoiri | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|0.4|N|131|22|25.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP}} | access = {{ill|Hōhi Main Line|ja|豊肥本線|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Bungotaketa Station|ja|豊後竹田駅|qq=draft}} via {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft}} (40–50 minutes) | hot spring type = {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft}} (Calcium bicarbonate spring), {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft}} | temperature = 40–45°C }}
[[File:Nagayu Onsen Ganiyu 1950s.jpg|thumb|right|Ganiyu (circa 1950)]]
'''Nagayu Onsen''' (長湯温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Taketa|ja|竹田市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft}}, in the former province of {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国|qq=draft}}. Due to its high concentration of {{ill|carbon dioxide|ja|炭酸|qq=draft}}, its abundant water flow, and optimal temperature, it is regarded as "one of the world's foremost carbonated springs"<ref>[http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen.com] Naoiri Town Tourism Association</ref> and "one of Japan's premier carbonated springs."<ref name="plan_takeda">{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/plan_takeda.pdf Taketa Hot Springs Group (Nagayu Onsen, Kuju Highland Onsen, Taketa-Ogi Onsen) National Health Resort Plan]}} Ministry of the Environment, May 2015</ref> It is particularly known for its health benefits, including for drinking therapy.
== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft}}<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Number of sources: 36<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Temperature: 24.6°C–52°C<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Water discharge: 16.1–290 liters per minute<ref name="plan_takeda" />
{{ill|Bicarbonate springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft}} can range in color from transparent to yellowish-white, depending on the source, while {{ill|Carbon dioxide springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft}} are generally colorless. Most facilities use a {{ill|free-flowing hot spring|ja|掛け流し|1=Kakenagashi|2=ja|3=掛け流し|4=zh|5=掛け流し|lt=Kakenagashi (flowing baths)|6=de|7=Kakenagashi|8=fr|9=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|qq=draft}} system.<ref name="onsen">[http://www.gozenyu.com/onsen.html Hot Spring Information] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref>
A study conducted in 1985 by {{ill|Kao Corporation|ja|花王|qq=draft}} identified Nagayu Onsen as one of Japan's leading carbonated springs.<ref name="plan_takeda" /><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012301.htm |title=Nagayu Story <1> Kao's Carbonated Spring Evaluation...Revitalizing the Town and Increasing Tourism Elevenfold |date=20100820235256}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 3, 2008</ref>
In 2006, the claim of being "Japan’s No.1 carbonated spring" was challenged due to insufficient scientific evidence, leading to a temporary retraction. However, a later evaluation in 2007 determined that while its CO₂ concentration was moderate, it had the highest discharge rate and optimal bathing temperature, leading to a reassertion of its claim.<ref name="na_08012305">{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012305.htm |title=Nagayu Story <5> "Japan’s Best" Reaffirmed...A Chance to Reevaluate Ourselves |date=20100826025904}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 8, 2008</ref>
== Medical Benefits ==
Although individual results may vary, the following benefits are attributed to Nagayu Onsen:
* Bathing: Beneficial for {{ill|diabetes|ja|糖尿病|qq=draft}}, {{ill|gastrointestinal diseases|ja|胃腸病|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|cardiovascular diseases|ja|心臓病|qq=draft}} * Drinking: Supports internal organ function<ref name="plan_takeda" />
== Onsen Town ==
[[File:Gozenyu.jpg|right|upright|thumb|Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"]] [[File:RamuneOnsenExterior.JPG|right|thumb|Ramune Onsen]]
The hot spring town is situated along the Serikawa River, surrounded by a rural landscape. Traditional inns and public bathhouses are scattered throughout the area.
=== Major Bathhouses ===
* '''Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"''' – Modern bathhouse built to resemble a European-style facility. Inspired by Nagayu’s sister-city partnership with Bad Krozingen, Germany. * '''Ramune Onsen''' – Known for its transparent, highly carbonated water. Designed by architect {{ill|Terunobu Fujimori|ja|藤森照信|qq=draft}}. * '''Ganiyu''' – Free outdoor bath visible from nearby roads and inns. * '''Choseiyu''' – Affordable public bath with an entrance fee of only 200 yen.
== History ==
* Nagayu Onsen is mentioned in the early 8th-century {{ill|Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|豊後国風土記|qq=draft}}.<ref name="plan_takeda" /> * 1706: A tea house for the {{ill|Oka Domain|ja|岡藩|qq=draft}} lord was constructed at the onsen.<ref name="nagayu_rekishi">[http://www.gozenyu.com/nagayu_rekishi.html History of Nagayu Onsen] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref> * 1978: Officially designated as a {{ill|National Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft}}. * 2015: Expanded designation as part of the {{ill|Taketa Hot Spring Group|ja|竹田温泉群|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/list.pdf National Health Resort List]}} Ministry of the Environment</ref>
== Access ==
* {{ill|Hōhi Main Line|ja|豊肥本線|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Bungotaketa Station|ja|豊後竹田駅|qq=draft}}, then {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft}} (40–50 minutes). * Limited bus services from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|阿蘇くまもと空港|qq=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of hot springs in Japan|ja|日本の温泉地一覧|1=List of hot springs in Japan|2=ja|3=日本の温泉地一覧|4=zh|5=日本溫泉列表|1=List of hot springs in Japan|2=ja|3=日本の温泉地一覧|4=zh|5=日本溫泉列表|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Carbon Dioxide Springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen Official Website] (Japanese)
[[Category:translated pages without meta categories]] {{translated page|ja|長湯温泉|version=104645983|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox hot spring | name = Nagayu Onsen | image = Ganiyu.JPG | caption = Ganiyu by the river {{Location map+|Japan Oita Prefecture|relief=1|width=300|caption=|float=center|places= {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 04| lat_sec = 0.4|lon_deg = 131| lon_min = 22| lon_sec = 25.4|position=right|label=Nagayu Onsen|label_size=100|caption=}} {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg=33|lat_min=05|lat_sec=09|lon_deg=131|lon_min=14|lon_sec=56|position=left|mark=3-fold rotation axis.svg|label={{ill|Mount Kujū|ja|九重山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kujū|qq=}}}} }} | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Taketa|ja|竹田市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taketa|qq=}}, Naoiri | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|0.4|N|131|22|25.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP}} | access = {{ill|Hōhi Main Line|ja|豊肥本線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōhi Main Line|qq=}}, {{ill|Bungotaketa Station|ja|豊後竹田駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungotaketa Station|qq=}} via {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes) | hot spring type = {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} (Calcium bicarbonate spring), {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} | temperature = 40–45°C }}
[[File:Nagayu Onsen Ganiyu 1950s.jpg|thumb|right|Ganiyu (circa 1950)]]
'''Nagayu Onsen''' (長湯温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Taketa|ja|竹田市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taketa|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Prefecture|qq=}}, in the former province of {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungo Province|qq=}}. Due to its high concentration of {{ill|carbon dioxide|ja|炭酸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/carbon dioxide|qq=}}, its abundant water flow, and optimal temperature, it is regarded as "one of the world's foremost carbonated springs"<ref>[http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen.com] Naoiri Town Tourism Association</ref> and "one of Japan's premier carbonated springs."<ref name="plan_takeda">{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/plan_takeda.pdf Taketa Hot Springs Group (Nagayu Onsen, Kuju Highland Onsen, Taketa-Ogi Onsen) National Health Resort Plan]}} Ministry of the Environment, May 2015</ref> It is particularly known for its health benefits, including for drinking therapy.
== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Number of sources: 36<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Temperature: 24.6°C–52°C<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Water discharge: 16.1–290 liters per minute<ref name="plan_takeda" />
{{ill|Bicarbonate springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} can range in color from transparent to yellowish-white, depending on the source, while {{ill|Carbon dioxide springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} are generally colorless. Most facilities use a {{ill|free-flowing hot spring|ja|掛け流し|1=Kakenagashi|2=ja|3=掛け流し|4=zh|5=掛け流し|lt=Kakenagashi (flowing baths)|6=de|7=Kakenagashi|8=fr|9=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/free-flowing hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakenagashi|qq=}} system.<ref name="onsen">[http://www.gozenyu.com/onsen.html Hot Spring Information] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref>
A study conducted in 1985 by {{ill|Kao Corporation|ja|花王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kao Corporation|qq=}} identified Nagayu Onsen as one of Japan's leading carbonated springs.<ref name="plan_takeda" /><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012301.htm |title=Nagayu Story <1> Kao's Carbonated Spring Evaluation...Revitalizing the Town and Increasing Tourism Elevenfold |date=20100820235256}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 3, 2008</ref>
In 2006, the claim of being "Japan’s No.1 carbonated spring" was challenged due to insufficient scientific evidence, leading to a temporary retraction. However, a later evaluation in 2007 determined that while its CO₂ concentration was moderate, it had the highest discharge rate and optimal bathing temperature, leading to a reassertion of its claim.<ref name="na_08012305">{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012305.htm |title=Nagayu Story <5> "Japan’s Best" Reaffirmed...A Chance to Reevaluate Ourselves |date=20100826025904}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 8, 2008</ref>
== Medical Benefits ==
Although individual results may vary, the following benefits are attributed to Nagayu Onsen:
* Bathing: Beneficial for {{ill|diabetes|ja|糖尿病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diabetes|qq=}}, {{ill|gastrointestinal diseases|ja|胃腸病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gastrointestinal diseases|qq=}}, and {{ill|cardiovascular diseases|ja|心臓病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cardiovascular diseases|qq=}} * Drinking: Supports internal organ function<ref name="plan_takeda" />
== Onsen Town ==
[[File:Gozenyu.jpg|right|upright|thumb|Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"]] [[File:RamuneOnsenExterior.JPG|right|thumb|Ramune Onsen]]
The hot spring town is situated along the Serikawa River, surrounded by a rural landscape. Traditional inns and public bathhouses are scattered throughout the area.
=== Major Bathhouses ===
* '''Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"''' – Modern bathhouse built to resemble a European-style facility. Inspired by Nagayu’s sister-city partnership with Bad Krozingen, Germany. * '''Ramune Onsen''' – Known for its transparent, highly carbonated water. Designed by architect {{ill|Terunobu Fujimori|ja|藤森照信|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terunobu Fujimori|qq=}}. * '''Ganiyu''' – Free outdoor bath visible from nearby roads and inns. * '''Choseiyu''' – Affordable public bath with an entrance fee of only 200 yen.
== History ==
* Nagayu Onsen is mentioned in the early 8th-century {{ill|Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|豊後国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}.<ref name="plan_takeda" /> * 1706: A tea house for the {{ill|Oka Domain|ja|岡藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oka Domain|qq=}} lord was constructed at the onsen.<ref name="nagayu_rekishi">[http://www.gozenyu.com/nagayu_rekishi.html History of Nagayu Onsen] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref> * 1978: Officially designated as a {{ill|National Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Health Resort|qq=}}. * 2015: Expanded designation as part of the {{ill|Taketa Hot Spring Group|ja|竹田温泉群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taketa Hot Spring Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/list.pdf National Health Resort List]}} Ministry of the Environment</ref>
== Access ==
* {{ill|Hōhi Main Line|ja|豊肥本線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōhi Main Line|qq=}} to {{ill|Bungotaketa Station|ja|豊後竹田駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungotaketa Station|qq=}}, then {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes). * Limited bus services from {{ill|Ōita Station|ja|大分駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Station|qq=}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|阿蘇くまもと空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of hot springs in Japan|ja|日本の温泉地一覧|1=List of hot springs in Japan|2=ja|3=日本の温泉地一覧|4=zh|5=日本溫泉列表|1=List of hot springs in Japan|2=ja|3=日本の温泉地一覧|4=zh|5=日本溫泉列表|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of hot springs in Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/List of hot springs in Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Carbon Dioxide Springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen Official Website] (Japanese)

{{Infobox hot spring | name = Nagayu Onsen | image = Ganiyu.JPG | caption = Ganiyu by the river {{Location map+|Japan Oita Prefecture|relief=1|width=300|caption=|float=center|places= {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 04| lat_sec = 0.4|lon_deg = 131| lon_min = 22| lon_sec = 25.4|position=right|label=Nagayu Onsen|label_size=100|caption=}} {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg=33|lat_min=05|lat_sec=09|lon_deg=131|lon_min=14|lon_sec=56|position=left|mark=3-fold rotation axis.svg|label=[[:en:Mount Kujū|Mount Kujū]]}} }} | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], [[:en:Taketa, Ōita|Taketa]], Naoiri | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|0.4|N|131|22|25.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP}} | access = [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]], [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]] via {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes) | hot spring type = {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} (Calcium bicarbonate spring), {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} | temperature = 40–45°C }}
[[File:Nagayu Onsen Ganiyu 1950s.jpg|thumb|right|Ganiyu (circa 1950)]]
'''Nagayu Onsen''' (長湯温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Taketa, Ōita|Taketa]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], in the former province of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Due to its high concentration of [[:en:Carbonic acid|carbon dioxide]], its abundant water flow, and optimal temperature, it is regarded as "one of the world's foremost carbonated springs"<ref>[http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen.com] Naoiri Town Tourism Association</ref> and "one of Japan's premier carbonated springs."<ref name="plan_takeda">{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/plan_takeda.pdf Taketa Hot Springs Group (Nagayu Onsen, Kuju Highland Onsen, Taketa-Ogi Onsen) National Health Resort Plan]}} Ministry of the Environment, May 2015</ref> It is particularly known for its health benefits, including for drinking therapy.
== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Number of sources: 36<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Temperature: 24.6°C–52°C<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Water discharge: 16.1–290 liters per minute<ref name="plan_takeda" />
{{ill|Bicarbonate springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} can range in color from transparent to yellowish-white, depending on the source, while {{ill|Carbon dioxide springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} are generally colorless. Most facilities use a {{ill|free-flowing hot spring|ja|掛け流し|1=Kakenagashi|2=ja|3=掛け流し|4=zh|5=掛け流し|lt=Kakenagashi (flowing baths)|6=de|7=Kakenagashi|8=fr|9=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/free-flowing hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakenagashi|qq=}} system.<ref name="onsen">[http://www.gozenyu.com/onsen.html Hot Spring Information] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref>
A study conducted in 1985 by [[:en:Kao Corporation|Kao Corporation]] identified Nagayu Onsen as one of Japan's leading carbonated springs.<ref name="plan_takeda" /><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012301.htm |title=Nagayu Story <1> Kao's Carbonated Spring Evaluation...Revitalizing the Town and Increasing Tourism Elevenfold |date=20100820235256}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 3, 2008</ref>
In 2006, the claim of being "Japan’s No.1 carbonated spring" was challenged due to insufficient scientific evidence, leading to a temporary retraction. However, a later evaluation in 2007 determined that while its CO₂ concentration was moderate, it had the highest discharge rate and optimal bathing temperature, leading to a reassertion of its claim.<ref name="na_08012305">{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012305.htm |title=Nagayu Story <5> "Japan’s Best" Reaffirmed...A Chance to Reevaluate Ourselves |date=20100826025904}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 8, 2008</ref>
== Medical Benefits ==
Although individual results may vary, the following benefits are attributed to Nagayu Onsen:
* Bathing: Beneficial for [[:en:Diabetes|diabetes]], {{ill|gastrointestinal diseases|ja|胃腸病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gastrointestinal diseases|qq=}}, and {{ill|cardiovascular diseases|ja|心臓病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cardiovascular diseases|qq=}} * Drinking: Supports internal organ function<ref name="plan_takeda" />
== Onsen Town ==
[[File:Gozenyu.jpg|right|upright|thumb|Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"]] [[File:RamuneOnsenExterior.JPG|right|thumb|Ramune Onsen]]
The hot spring town is situated along the Serikawa River, surrounded by a rural landscape. Traditional inns and public bathhouses are scattered throughout the area.
=== Major Bathhouses ===
* '''Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"''' – Modern bathhouse built to resemble a European-style facility. Inspired by Nagayu’s sister-city partnership with Bad Krozingen, Germany. * '''Ramune Onsen''' – Known for its transparent, highly carbonated water. Designed by architect [[:en:Terunobu Fujimori|Terunobu Fujimori]]. * '''Ganiyu''' – Free outdoor bath visible from nearby roads and inns. * '''Choseiyu''' – Affordable public bath with an entrance fee of only 200 yen.
== History ==
* Nagayu Onsen is mentioned in the early 8th-century {{ill|Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|豊後国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}.<ref name="plan_takeda" /> * 1706: A tea house for the [[:en:Oka Domain|Oka Domain]] lord was constructed at the onsen.<ref name="nagayu_rekishi">[http://www.gozenyu.com/nagayu_rekishi.html History of Nagayu Onsen] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref> * 1978: Officially designated as a {{ill|National Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Health Resort|qq=}}. * 2015: Expanded designation as part of the {{ill|Taketa Hot Spring Group|ja|竹田温泉群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taketa Hot Spring Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/list.pdf National Health Resort List]}} Ministry of the Environment</ref>
== Access ==
* [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]] to [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]], then {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes). * Limited bus services from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]] and {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|阿蘇くまもと空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:List of hot springs in Japan|List of hot springs in Japan]] * {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Carbon Dioxide Springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}

Most facilities use a {{ill|free-flowing hot spring|ja|掛け流し|1=Kakenagashi|2=ja|3=掛け流し|4=zh|5=掛け流し|lt=Kakenagashi (flowing baths)|6=de|7=Kakenagashi|8=fr|9=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/free-flowing hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakenagashi|qq=}} system.<ref name="onsen">[http://www.gozenyu.com/onsen.html Hot Spring Information] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref>
A study conducted in 1985 by [[:en:Kao Corporation|Kao Corporation]] identified Nagayu Onsen as one of Japan's leading carbonated springs.<ref name="plan_takeda" /><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012301.htm |title=Nagayu Story <1> Kao's Carbonated Spring Evaluation...Revitalizing the Town and Increasing Tourism Elevenfold |date=20100820235256}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 3, 2008</ref>
In 2006, the claim of being "Japan’s No.1 carbonated spring" was challenged due to insufficient scientific evidence, leading to a temporary retraction. However, a later evaluation in 2007 determined that while its CO₂ concentration was moderate, it had the highest discharge rate and optimal bathing temperature, leading to a reassertion of its claim.<ref name="na_08012305">{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012305.htm |title=Nagayu Story <5> "Japan’s Best" Reaffirmed...A Chance to Reevaluate Ourselves |date=20100826025904}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 8, 2008</ref>
== Medical Benefits ==
Although individual results may vary, the following benefits are attributed to Nagayu Onsen:
* Bathing: Beneficial for [[:en:Diabetes|diabetes]], {{ill|gastrointestinal diseases|ja|胃腸病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gastrointestinal diseases|qq=}}, and {{ill|cardiovascular diseases|ja|心臓病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cardiovascular diseases|qq=}} * Drinking: Supports internal organ function<ref name="plan_takeda" />
== Onsen Town ==
[[File:Gozenyu.jpg|right|upright|thumb|Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"]] [[File:RamuneOnsenExterior.JPG|right|thumb|Ramune Onsen]]
The hot spring town is situated along the Serikawa River, surrounded by a rural landscape. Traditional inns and public bathhouses are scattered throughout the area.
=== Major Bathhouses ===
* '''Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"''' – Modern bathhouse built to resemble a European-style facility. Inspired by Nagayu’s sister-city partnership with Bad Krozingen, Germany. * '''Ramune Onsen''' – Known for its transparent, highly carbonated water. Designed by architect [[:en:Terunobu Fujimori|Terunobu Fujimori]]. * '''Ganiyu''' – Free outdoor bath visible from nearby roads and inns. * '''Choseiyu''' – Affordable public bath with an entrance fee of only 200 yen.
== History ==
* Nagayu Onsen is mentioned in the early 8th-century {{ill|Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|豊後国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}.<ref name="plan_takeda" /> * 1706: A tea house for the [[:en:Oka Domain|Oka Domain]] lord was constructed at the onsen.<ref name="nagayu_rekishi">[http://www.gozenyu.com/nagayu_rekishi.html History of Nagayu Onsen] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref> * 1978: Officially designated as a {{ill|National Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Health Resort|qq=}}. * 2015: Expanded designation as part of the {{ill|Taketa Hot Spring Group|ja|竹田温泉群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taketa Hot Spring Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/list.pdf National Health Resort List]}} Ministry of the Environment</ref>
== Access ==
* [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]] to [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]], then {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes). * Limited bus services from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]] and {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|阿蘇くまもと空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:List of hot springs in Japan|List of hot springs in Japan]] * {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Carbon Dioxide Springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen Official Website] (Japanese)

revision.text:
{{Infobox hot spring | name = Nagayu Onsen | image = Ganiyu.JPG | caption = Ganiyu by the river {{Location map+|Japan Oita Prefecture|relief=1|width=300|caption=|float=center|places= {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 04| lat_sec = 0.4|lon_deg = 131| lon_min = 22| lon_sec = 25.4|position=right|label=Nagayu Onsen|label_size=100|caption=}} {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg=33|lat_min=05|lat_sec=09|lon_deg=131|lon_min=14|lon_sec=56|position=left|mark=3-fold rotation axis.svg|label=[[:en:Mount Kujū|Mount Kujū]]}} }} | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], [[:en:Taketa, Ōita|Taketa]], Naoiri | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|0.4|N|131|22|25.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP}} | access = [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]], [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]] via {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes) | hot spring type = {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} (Calcium bicarbonate spring), {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} | temperature = 40–45°C }}
[[File:Nagayu Onsen Ganiyu 1950s.jpg|thumb|right|Ganiyu (circa 1950)]]
'''Nagayu Onsen''' (長湯温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Taketa, Ōita|Taketa]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], in the former province of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Due to its high concentration of [[:en:Carbonic acid|carbon dioxide]], its abundant water flow, and optimal temperature, it is regarded as "one of the world's foremost carbonated springs"<ref>[http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen.com] Naoiri Town Tourism Association</ref> and "one of Japan's premier carbonated springs."<ref name="plan_takeda">{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/plan_takeda.pdf Taketa Hot Springs Group (Nagayu Onsen, Kuju Highland Onsen, Taketa-Ogi Onsen) National Health Resort Plan]}} Ministry of the Environment, May 2015</ref> It is particularly known for its health benefits, including for drinking therapy.
== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Number of sources: 36<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Temperature: 24.6°C–52°C<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Water discharge: 16.1–290 liters per minute<ref name="plan_takeda" />
{{ill|Bicarbonate springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} can range in color from transparent to yellowish-white, depending on the source, while {{ill|Carbon dioxide springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} are generally colorless. Most facilities use a {{ill|free-flowing hot spring|ja|掛け流し|1=Kakenagashi|2=ja|3=掛け流し|4=zh|5=掛け流し|lt=Kakenagashi (flowing baths)|6=de|7=Kakenagashi|8=fr|9=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/free-flowing hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakenagashi|qq=}} system.<ref name="onsen">[http://www.gozenyu.com/onsen.html Hot Spring Information] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref>
A study conducted in 1985 by [[:en:Kao Corporation|Kao Corporation]] identified Nagayu Onsen as one of Japan's leading carbonated springs.<ref name="plan_takeda" /><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012301.htm |title=Nagayu Story <1> Kao's Carbonated Spring Evaluation...Revitalizing the Town and Increasing Tourism Elevenfold |date=20100820235256}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 3, 2008</ref>
In 2006, the claim of being "Japan’s No.1 carbonated spring" was challenged due to insufficient scientific evidence, leading to a temporary retraction. However, a later evaluation in 2007 determined that while its CO₂ concentration was moderate, it had the highest discharge rate and optimal bathing temperature, leading to a reassertion of its claim.<ref name="na_08012305">{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012305.htm |title=Nagayu Story <5> "Japan’s Best" Reaffirmed...A Chance to Reevaluate Ourselves |date=20100826025904}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 8, 2008</ref>
== Medical Benefits ==
Although individual results may vary, the following benefits are attributed to Nagayu Onsen:
* Bathing: Beneficial for [[:en:Diabetes|diabetes]], {{ill|gastrointestinal diseases|ja|胃腸病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gastrointestinal diseases|qq=}}, and {{ill|cardiovascular diseases|ja|心臓病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cardiovascular diseases|qq=}} * Drinking: Supports internal organ function<ref name="plan_takeda" />
== Onsen Town ==
[[File:Gozenyu.jpg|right|upright|thumb|Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"]] [[File:RamuneOnsenExterior.JPG|right|thumb|Ramune Onsen]]
The hot spring town is situated along the Serikawa River, surrounded by a rural landscape. Traditional inns and public bathhouses are scattered throughout the area.
=== Major Bathhouses ===
* '''Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"''' – Modern bathhouse built to resemble a European-style facility. Inspired by Nagayu’s sister-city partnership with Bad Krozingen, Germany. * '''Ramune Onsen''' – Known for its transparent, highly carbonated water. Designed by architect [[:en:Terunobu Fujimori|Terunobu Fujimori]]. * '''Ganiyu''' – Free outdoor bath visible from nearby roads and inns. * '''Choseiyu''' – Affordable public bath with an entrance fee of only 200 yen.
== History ==
* Nagayu Onsen is mentioned in the early 8th-century {{ill|Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|豊後国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}.<ref name="plan_takeda" /> * 1706: A tea house for the [[:en:Oka Domain|Oka Domain]] lord was constructed at the onsen.<ref name="nagayu_rekishi">[http://www.gozenyu.com/nagayu_rekishi.html History of Nagayu Onsen] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref> * 1978: Officially designated as a {{ill|National Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Health Resort|qq=}}. * 2015: Expanded designation as part of the {{ill|Taketa Hot Spring Group|ja|竹田温泉群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taketa Hot Spring Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/list.pdf National Health Resort List]}} Ministry of the Environment</ref>
== Access ==
* [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]] to [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]], then {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes). * Limited bus services from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]] and {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|阿蘇くまもと空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}.

== Related Articles == * [[:en:List of hot springs in Japan|List of hot springs in Japan]] * {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Carbon Dioxide Springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox hot spring | name = Nagayu Onsen | image = Ganiyu.JPG | caption = Ganiyu by the river {{Location map+|Japan Oita Prefecture|relief=1|width=300|caption=|float=center|places= {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 04| lat_sec = 0.4|lon_deg = 131| lon_min = 22| lon_sec = 25.4|position=right|label=Nagayu Onsen|label_size=100|caption=}} {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg=33|lat_min=05|lat_sec=09|lon_deg=131|lon_min=14|lon_sec=56|position=left|mark=3-fold rotation axis.svg|label=[[:en:Mount Kujū|Mount Kujū]]}} }} | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], [[:en:Taketa, Ōita|Taketa]], Naoiri | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|0.4|N|131|22|25.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP}} | access = [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]], [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]] via {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes) | hot spring type = {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} (Calcium bicarbonate spring), {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} | temperature = 40–45°C }}
[[File:Nagayu Onsen Ganiyu 1950s.jpg|thumb|right|Ganiyu (circa 1950)]]
'''Nagayu Onsen''' (長湯温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Taketa, Ōita|Taketa]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], in the former province of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Due to its high concentration of [[:en:Carbonic acid|carbon dioxide]], its abundant water flow, and optimal temperature, it is regarded as "one of the world's foremost carbonated springs"<ref>[http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen.com] Naoiri Town Tourism Association</ref> and "one of Japan's premier carbonated springs."<ref name="plan_takeda">{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/plan_takeda.pdf Taketa Hot Springs Group (Nagayu Onsen, Kuju Highland Onsen, Taketa-Ogi Onsen) National Health Resort Plan]}} Ministry of the Environment, May 2015</ref> It is particularly known for its health benefits, including for drinking therapy.
== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Number of sources: 36<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Temperature: 24.6°C–52°C<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Water discharge: 16.1–290 liters per minute<ref name="plan_takeda" />
{{ill|Bicarbonate springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} can range in color from transparent to yellowish-white, depending on the source, while {{ill|Carbon dioxide springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} are generally colorless. Most facilities use a {{ill|free-flowing hot spring|ja|掛け流し|1=Kakenagashi|2=ja|3=掛け流し|4=zh|5=掛け流し|lt=Kakenagashi (flowing baths)|6=de|7=Kakenagashi|8=fr|9=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/free-flowing hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakenagashi|qq=}} system.<ref name="onsen">[http://www.gozenyu.com/onsen.html Hot Spring Information] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref>
A study conducted in 1985 by [[:en:Kao Corporation|Kao Corporation]] identified Nagayu Onsen as one of Japan's leading carbonated springs.<ref name="plan_takeda" /><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012301.htm |title=Nagayu Story <1> Kao's Carbonated Spring Evaluation...Revitalizing the Town and Increasing Tourism Elevenfold |date=20100820235256}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 3, 2008</ref>
In 2006, the claim of being "Japan’s No.1 carbonated spring" was challenged due to insufficient scientific evidence, leading to a temporary retraction. However, a later evaluation in 2007 determined that while its CO₂ concentration was moderate, it had the highest discharge rate and optimal bathing temperature, leading to a reassertion of its claim.<ref name="na_08012305">{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012305.htm |title=Nagayu Story <5> "Japan’s Best" Reaffirmed...A Chance to Reevaluate Ourselves |date=20100826025904}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 8, 2008</ref>
== Medical Benefits ==
Although individual results may vary, the following benefits are attributed to Nagayu Onsen:
* Bathing: Beneficial for [[:en:Diabetes|diabetes]], {{ill|gastrointestinal diseases|ja|胃腸病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gastrointestinal diseases|qq=}}, and {{ill|cardiovascular diseases|ja|心臓病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cardiovascular diseases|qq=}} * Drinking: Supports internal organ function<ref name="plan_takeda" />
== Onsen Town ==
[[File:Gozenyu.jpg|right|upright|thumb|Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"]] [[File:RamuneOnsenExterior.JPG|right|thumb|Ramune Onsen]]
The hot spring town is situated along the Serikawa River, surrounded by a rural landscape. Traditional inns and public bathhouses are scattered throughout the area.
=== Major Bathhouses ===
* '''Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"''' – Modern bathhouse built to resemble a European-style facility. Inspired by Nagayu’s sister-city partnership with Bad Krozingen, Germany. * '''Ramune Onsen''' – Known for its transparent, highly carbonated water. Designed by architect [[:en:Terunobu Fujimori|Terunobu Fujimori]]. * '''Ganiyu''' – Free outdoor bath visible from nearby roads and inns. * '''Choseiyu''' – Affordable public bath with an entrance fee of only 200 yen.
== History ==
* Nagayu Onsen is mentioned in the early 8th-century {{ill|Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|豊後国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}.<ref name="plan_takeda" /> * 1706: A tea house for the [[:en:Oka Domain|Oka Domain]] lord was constructed at the onsen.<ref name="nagayu_rekishi">[http://www.gozenyu.com/nagayu_rekishi.html History of Nagayu Onsen] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref> * 1978: Officially designated as a {{ill|National Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Health Resort|qq=}}. * 2015: Expanded designation as part of the {{ill|Taketa Hot Spring Group|ja|竹田温泉群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taketa Hot Spring Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/list.pdf National Health Resort List]}} Ministry of the Environment</ref>
== Access ==
* [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]] to [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]], then {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes). * Limited bus services from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]] and {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|阿蘇くまもと空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:List of hot springs in Japan|List of hot springs in Japan]] * {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Carbon Dioxide Springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen Official Website] (Japanese)

Inspired by Nagayu’s sister-city partnership with Bad Krozingen, Germany. * '''Ramune Onsen''' – Known for its transparent, highly carbonated water. Designed by architect [[:en:Terunobu Fujimori|Terunobu Fujimori]]. * '''Ganiyu''' – Free outdoor bath visible from nearby roads and inns. * '''Choseiyu''' – Affordable public bath with an entrance fee of only 200 yen.
== History ==
* Nagayu Onsen is mentioned in the early 8th-century {{ill|Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|豊後国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}.<ref name="plan_takeda" /> * 1706: A tea house for the [[:en:Oka Domain|Oka Domain]] lord was constructed at the onsen.<ref name="nagayu_rekishi">[http://www.gozenyu.com/nagayu_rekishi.html History of Nagayu Onsen] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref> * 1978: Officially designated as a {{ill|National Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Health Resort|qq=}}. * 2015: Expanded designation as part of the {{ill|Taketa Hot Spring Group|ja|竹田温泉群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taketa Hot Spring Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/list.pdf National Health Resort List]}} Ministry of the Environment</ref>
== Access ==
* [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]] to [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]], then {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes). * Limited bus services from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]] and {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|阿蘇くまもと空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:List of hot springs in Japan|List of hot springs in Japan]] * {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Carbon Dioxide Springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen Official Website] (Japanese)

[[ja:長湯温泉]] [[Category:国民保養温泉地]] [[Category:Hot springs of Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Taketa, Oita]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Nagayu Onsen}}

{{Infobox hot spring | name = Nagayu Onsen | image = Ganiyu.JPG | caption = Ganiyu by the river {{Location map+|Japan Oita Prefecture|relief=1|width=300|caption=|float=center|places= {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg = 33| lat_min = 04| lat_sec = 0.4|lon_deg = 131| lon_min = 22| lon_sec = 25.4|position=right|label=Nagayu Onsen|label_size=100|caption=}} {{Location map~|Japan Oita Prefecture|lat_deg=33|lat_min=05|lat_sec=09|lon_deg=131|lon_min=14|lon_sec=56|position=left|mark=3-fold rotation axis.svg|label=[[:en:Mount Kujū|Mount Kujū]]}} }} | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], [[:en:Taketa, Ōita|Taketa]], Naoiri | coordinates = {{coord|33|04|0.4|N|131|22|25.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP}} | access = [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]], [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]] via {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes) | hot spring type = {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} (Calcium bicarbonate spring), {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} | temperature = 40–45°C }}
[[File:Nagayu Onsen Ganiyu 1950s.jpg|thumb|right|Ganiyu (circa 1950)]]
'''Nagayu Onsen''' (長湯温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Taketa, Ōita|Taketa]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], in the former province of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Due to its high concentration of [[:en:Carbonic acid|carbon dioxide]], its abundant water flow, and optimal temperature, it is regarded as "one of the world's foremost carbonated springs"<ref>[http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen.com] Naoiri Town Tourism Association</ref> and "one of Japan's premier carbonated springs."<ref name="plan_takeda">{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/plan_takeda.pdf Taketa Hot Springs Group (Nagayu Onsen, Kuju Highland Onsen, Taketa-Ogi Onsen) National Health Resort Plan]}} Ministry of the Environment, May 2015</ref> It is particularly known for its health benefits, including for drinking therapy.
== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Number of sources: 36<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Temperature: 24.6°C–52°C<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Water discharge: 16.1–290 liters per minute<ref name="plan_takeda" />
{{ill|Bicarbonate springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} can range in color from transparent to yellowish-white, depending on the source, while {{ill|Carbon dioxide springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} are generally colorless. Most facilities use a {{ill|free-flowing hot spring|ja|掛け流し|1=Kakenagashi|2=ja|3=掛け流し|4=zh|5=掛け流し|lt=Kakenagashi (flowing baths)|6=de|7=Kakenagashi|8=fr|9=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/free-flowing hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakenagashi|qq=}} system.<ref name="onsen">[http://www.gozenyu.com/onsen.html Hot Spring Information] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref>
A study conducted in 1985 by [[:en:Kao Corporation|Kao Corporation]] identified Nagayu Onsen as one of Japan's leading carbonated springs.<ref name="plan_takeda" /><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012301.htm |title=Nagayu Story <1> Kao's Carbonated Spring Evaluation...Revitalizing the Town and Increasing Tourism Elevenfold |date=20100820235256}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 3, 2008</ref>
In 2006, the claim of being "Japan’s No.1 carbonated spring" was challenged due to insufficient scientific evidence, leading to a temporary retraction. However, a later evaluation in 2007 determined that while its CO₂ concentration was moderate, it had the highest discharge rate and optimal bathing temperature, leading to a reassertion of its claim.<ref name="na_08012305">{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012305.htm |title=Nagayu Story <5> "Japan’s Best" Reaffirmed...A Chance to Reevaluate Ourselves |date=20100826025904}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 8, 2008</ref>
== Medical Benefits ==
Although individual results may vary, the following benefits are attributed to Nagayu Onsen:
* Bathing: Beneficial for [[:en:Diabetes|diabetes]], {{ill|gastrointestinal diseases|ja|胃腸病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gastrointestinal diseases|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消化器学}}, and {{ill|cardiovascular diseases|ja|心臓病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cardiovascular diseases|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * Drinking: Supports internal organ function<ref name="plan_takeda" />
== Onsen Town ==
[[File:Gozenyu.jpg|right|upright|thumb|Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"]] [[File:RamuneOnsenExterior.JPG|right|thumb|Ramune Onsen]]
The hot spring town is situated along the Serikawa River, surrounded by a rural landscape. Traditional inns and public bathhouses are scattered throughout the area.
=== Major Bathhouses ===
* '''Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"''' – Modern bathhouse built to resemble a European-style facility. Inspired by Nagayu’s sister-city partnership with Bad Krozingen, Germany. * '''Ramune Onsen''' – Known for its transparent, highly carbonated water. Designed by architect [[:en:Terunobu Fujimori|Terunobu Fujimori]]. * '''Ganiyu''' – Free outdoor bath visible from nearby roads and inns. * '''Choseiyu''' – Affordable public bath with an entrance fee of only 200 yen.
== History ==
* Nagayu Onsen is mentioned in the early 8th-century {{ill|Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|豊後国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.<ref name="plan_takeda" /> * 1706: A tea house for the [[:en:Oka Domain|Oka Domain]] lord was constructed at the onsen.<ref name="nagayu_rekishi">[http://www.gozenyu.com/nagayu_rekishi.html History of Nagayu Onsen] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref> * 1978: Officially designated as a {{ill|National Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Health Resort|qq=}}. * 2015: Expanded designation as part of the {{ill|Taketa Hot Spring Group|ja|竹田温泉群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taketa Hot Spring Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/list.pdf National Health Resort List]}} Ministry of the Environment</ref>
== Access ==
* [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]] to [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]], then {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes). * Limited bus services from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]] and {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|阿蘇くまもと空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊本空港}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:List of hot springs in Japan|List of hot springs in Japan]] * {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Carbon Dioxide Springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}

Due to its high concentration of [[:en:Carbonic acid|carbon dioxide]], its abundant water flow, and optimal temperature, it is regarded as "one of the world's foremost carbonated springs"<ref>[http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen.com] Naoiri Town Tourism Association</ref> and "one of Japan's premier carbonated springs."<ref name="plan_takeda">{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/plan_takeda.pdf Taketa Hot Springs Group (Nagayu Onsen, Kuju Highland Onsen, Taketa-Ogi Onsen) National Health Resort Plan]}} Ministry of the Environment, May 2015</ref> It is particularly known for its health benefits, including for drinking therapy.
== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Number of sources: 36<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Temperature: 24.6°C–52°C<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Water discharge: 16.1–290 liters per minute<ref name="plan_takeda" />
{{ill|Bicarbonate springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} can range in color from transparent to yellowish-white, depending on the source, while {{ill|Carbon dioxide springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} are generally colorless. Most facilities use a {{ill|free-flowing hot spring|ja|掛け流し|1=Kakenagashi|2=ja|3=掛け流し|4=zh|5=掛け流し|lt=Kakenagashi (flowing baths)|6=de|7=Kakenagashi|8=fr|9=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/free-flowing hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakenagashi|qq=}} system.<ref name="onsen">[http://www.gozenyu.com/onsen.html Hot Spring Information] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref>
A study conducted in 1985 by [[:en:Kao Corporation|Kao Corporation]] identified Nagayu Onsen as one of Japan's leading carbonated springs.<ref name="plan_takeda" /><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012301.htm |title=Nagayu Story <1> Kao's Carbonated Spring Evaluation...Revitalizing the Town and Increasing Tourism Elevenfold |date=20100820235256}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 3, 2008</ref>
In 2006, the claim of being "Japan’s No.1 carbonated spring" was challenged due to insufficient scientific evidence, leading to a temporary retraction. However, a later evaluation in 2007 determined that while its CO₂ concentration was moderate, it had the highest discharge rate and optimal bathing temperature, leading to a reassertion of its claim.<ref name="na_08012305">{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012305.htm |title=Nagayu Story <5> "Japan’s Best" Reaffirmed...A Chance to Reevaluate Ourselves |date=20100826025904}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 8, 2008</ref>
== Medical Benefits ==
Although individual results may vary, the following benefits are attributed to Nagayu Onsen:
* Bathing: Beneficial for [[:en:Diabetes|diabetes]], {{ill|gastrointestinal diseases|ja|胃腸病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gastrointestinal diseases|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消化器学}}, and {{ill|cardiovascular diseases|ja|心臓病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cardiovascular diseases|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * Drinking: Supports internal organ function<ref name="plan_takeda" />
== Onsen Town ==
[[File:Gozenyu.jpg|right|upright|thumb|Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"]] [[File:RamuneOnsenExterior.JPG|right|thumb|Ramune Onsen]]
The hot spring town is situated along the Serikawa River, surrounded by a rural landscape. Traditional inns and public bathhouses are scattered throughout the area.
=== Major Bathhouses ===
* '''Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"''' – Modern bathhouse built to resemble a European-style facility. Inspired by Nagayu’s sister-city partnership with Bad Krozingen, Germany. * '''Ramune Onsen''' – Known for its transparent, highly carbonated water. Designed by architect [[:en:Terunobu Fujimori|Terunobu Fujimori]]. * '''Ganiyu''' – Free outdoor bath visible from nearby roads and inns. * '''Choseiyu''' – Affordable public bath with an entrance fee of only 200 yen.
== History ==
* Nagayu Onsen is mentioned in the early 8th-century {{ill|Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|豊後国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.<ref name="plan_takeda" /> * 1706: A tea house for the [[:en:Oka Domain|Oka Domain]] lord was constructed at the onsen.<ref name="nagayu_rekishi">[http://www.gozenyu.com/nagayu_rekishi.html History of Nagayu Onsen] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref> * 1978: Officially designated as a {{ill|National Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Health Resort|qq=}}. * 2015: Expanded designation as part of the {{ill|Taketa Hot Spring Group|ja|竹田温泉群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taketa Hot Spring Group|qq=}}.<ref>{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/list.pdf National Health Resort List]}} Ministry of the Environment</ref>
== Access ==
* [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]] to [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]], then {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes). * Limited bus services from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]] and {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|阿蘇くまもと空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊本空港}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:List of hot springs in Japan|List of hot springs in Japan]] * {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Carbon Dioxide Springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen Official Website] (Japanese)

[[File:Nagayu Onsen Ganiyu 1950s.jpg|thumb|right|Ganiyu (circa 1950)]]
'''Nagayu Onsen''' (長湯温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Taketa, Ōita|Taketa]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], in the former province of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Due to its high concentration of [[:en:Carbonic acid|carbon dioxide]], its abundant water flow, and optimal temperature, it is regarded as "one of the world's foremost carbonated springs"<ref>[http://www.nagayu-onsen.com/ Nagayu Onsen.com] Naoiri Town Tourism Association</ref> and "one of Japan's premier carbonated springs."<ref name="plan_takeda">{{PDFlink|[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/plan_takeda.pdf Taketa Hot Springs Group (Nagayu Onsen, Kuju Highland Onsen, Taketa-Ogi Onsen) National Health Resort Plan]}} Ministry of the Environment, May 2015</ref> It is particularly known for its health benefits, including for drinking therapy.
== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Carbon dioxide spring|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Number of sources: 36<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Temperature: 24.6°C–52°C<ref name="plan_takeda" /> ** Water discharge: 16.1–290 liters per minute<ref name="plan_takeda" />
{{ill|Bicarbonate springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} can range in color from transparent to yellowish-white, depending on the source, while {{ill|Carbon dioxide springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon dioxide springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}} are generally colorless. Most facilities use a {{ill|free-flowing hot spring|ja|掛け流し|1=Kakenagashi|2=ja|3=掛け流し|4=zh|5=掛け流し|lt=Kakenagashi (flowing baths)|6=de|7=Kakenagashi|8=fr|9=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|1=Kakenagashi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/free-flowing hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakenagashi|qq=}} system.<ref name="onsen">[http://www.gozenyu.com/onsen.html Hot Spring Information] Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"</ref>
A study conducted in 1985 by [[:en:Kao Corporation|Kao Corporation]] identified Nagayu Onsen as one of Japan's leading carbonated springs.<ref name="plan_takeda" /><ref>{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012301.htm |title=Nagayu Story <1> Kao's Carbonated Spring Evaluation...Revitalizing the Town and Increasing Tourism Elevenfold |date=20100820235256}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 3, 2008</ref>
In 2006, the claim of being "Japan’s No.1 carbonated spring" was challenged due to insufficient scientific evidence, leading to a temporary retraction. However, a later evaluation in 2007 determined that while its CO₂ concentration was moderate, it had the highest discharge rate and optimal bathing temperature, leading to a reassertion of its claim.<ref name="na_08012305">{{Wayback |url=http://kyushu.yomiuri.co.jp/entame/onsen/nagayu/na_08012305.htm |title=Nagayu Story <5> "Japan’s Best" Reaffirmed...A Chance to Reevaluate Ourselves |date=20100826025904}} Yomiuri Shimbun, January 8, 2008</ref>
== Medical Benefits ==
Although individual results may vary, the following benefits are attributed to Nagayu Onsen:
* Bathing: Beneficial for [[:en:Diabetes|diabetes]], {{ill|gastrointestinal diseases|ja|胃腸病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gastrointestinal diseases|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消化器学}}, and {{ill|cardiovascular diseases|ja|心臓病|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cardiovascular diseases|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * Drinking: Supports internal organ function<ref name="plan_takeda" />
== Onsen Town ==
[[File:Gozenyu.jpg|right|upright|thumb|Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"]] [[File:RamuneOnsenExterior.JPG|right|thumb|Ramune Onsen]]
The hot spring town is situated along the Serikawa River, surrounded by a rural landscape. Traditional inns and public bathhouses are scattered throughout the area.
=== Major Bathhouses ===
* '''Nagayu Onsen Wellness Cultural Hall "Gozenyu"''' – Modern bathhouse built to resemble a European-style facility. Inspired by Nagayu’s sister-city partnership with Bad Krozingen, Germany. * '''Ramune Onsen''' – Known for its transparent, highly carbonated water. Designed by architect [[:en:Terunobu Fujimori|Terunobu Fujimori]]. * '''Ganiyu''' – Free outdoor bath visible from nearby roads and inns. * '''Choseiyu''' – Affordable public bath with an entrance fee of only 200 yen.
== History ==

* [[:en:Hōhi Main Line|Hōhi Main Line]] to [[:en:Bungo-Taketa Station|Bungotaketa Station]], then {{ill|Ōno Taketa Bus|ja|大野竹田バス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōno Taketa Bus|qq=}} (40–50 minutes). * Limited bus services from [[:en:Ōita Station|Ōita Station]] and {{ill|Kumamoto Airport|ja|阿蘇くまもと空港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Airport|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊本空港}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:List of hot springs in Japan|List of hot springs in Japan]] * {{ill|Bicarbonate spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Carbon Dioxide Springs|ja|二酸化炭素泉|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|1=Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Carbon Dioxide Springs|qq=}}
